questions.question: Find the discriminant $D$ of the quadratic equation $3x^2 - 5x + 2 = 0$ and state the nature of its roots.
questions.answer: $D = 1$; the roots are real and distinct.

steps: Identify the coefficients $a$, $b$, and $c$ by comparing the equation with the standard form $ax^2 + bx + c = 0$.
Calculate the discriminant using the formula $D = b^2 - 4ac$ and determine the nature of the roots based on whether $D > 0$, $D = 0$, or $D < 0$.
solution.derivation: a = 3, b = -5, c = 2 \ D = b^2 - 4ac \ D = (-5)^2 - 4(3)(2) \ D = 25 - 24 \ D = 1 \ \text{Since } D > 0, \text{ the roots are real and distinct.}

retrieved_sources: Chapter_3 (Pair of Linear Equations in Two Variables).pdf
